>  I don't think this is a public promise, the auth.globus.org token will be the "top level" one.

This is true. If an `auth.globus.org` is returned, it will always be the 'top level' one. This was done because the `id_token` contains an `at_hash` claim that can be used to validate the `access_token` returned alongside it. It would have been weird if the `id_token` at the top level contained a hash for one of the other access tokens buried in `other_tokens` and you had to find the matching one. It also would potentially have broken third-party clients expecting to easily validate against the top level token.

@Erotemic's comments on this matter are spot on. Here are some of my additional thoughts.

We could simply write our own `basic_gil_scoped_acquire/release` RAII classes that use the "basic" `PyGILState_*` API, but the biggest problem we're having is that there are places in pybind that use the advanced API, and these can't be changed without changing pybind. Most notable of these is the `PYBIND11_OVERLOAD()` macros and `error_already_set`. I was able to write "basic" versions of the `PYBIND11_OVERLOAD()` macros, but because `error_already_set` is used in so many places and is thrown any time Python code throws an exception, trying to make a new version of `error_already_set` would involve redefining so many classes and functions that it would be equivalent to rewriting pybind. At that point, it would be a lot simpler to just fork the project entirely. In order to solve this issue, we will need to make some kind of change to pybind - simply redefining the problematic classes and macros won't be enough.

In short, there are two issues here:

1. Come up with a way to select the desired GIL behavior for projects (such as ours) that don't want to use the advanced API and are negatively impacted by its use. (This is the higher priority concern.)
2. What should the default be? Basic or advanced? Personally, I agree with @Erotemic that the pybind default should be compatible with the CPython standard, but changing this default would require a pybind major version update, so it is understandable why there would be resistance to such a change. When I wrote #1286, I designed it with the specific intention of leaving the default behavior alone (use the pybind11 GIL API) and only use the `PyGILState_*` API if the developer explicitly selects it, in order to avoid breaking compatibility.

@Erotemic forwarded me this from @wjakob:

> Right now the solution looks unappealing to me due to the introduction of extra ‘options’ (which seems very heavy-handed). An optional define like PYBIND11_USE_PYTHON_GILSTATE that switches over to an alternative gil_scoped_acquire_basic / gil_scoped_release_basic RAII wapper may be more suitable.

Can you explain your thoughts here? What I'm thinking is that, at least to the end user, there isn't a great deal of difference between my proposal and adding a `#define`. Both would require code changes in the project using pybind to select what they want to do. There are only two major differences: my option adds a rather large amount of code whereas the `#define` would only be a few lines long (an understandable concern in any project), and the `#define` happens at compile time whereas my proposal with an option would happen at runtime. (@Erotemic pointed out to me that the runtime option would have a slight performance penalty since there would be a few extra branching instructions, but given that the GIL - and mutexes in general - already use expensive kernel system calls for lock contention, this doesn't seem like a major bottleneck to me.)

One concern that @jagerman had is that having a global runtime option could change the value for other libraries (if library A uses the proposed "basic" `PyGILState_*` API and library B uses the advanced API, one of them could clobber the other.) What I found during my testing is that this doesn't happen (in fact, since we have multiple shared objects in our project, I had to insert my option in more than one place.) This would make sense to me, because pybind11 is a header-only library, so each executable/shared library object would get its own copy of the static option variable, though relying on this behavior is not necessarily a good idea.

@Erotemic also came up with the idea of having both a runtime and compile-time option, where the compile-time option sets the default for the runtime option, but the runtime option can still be changed.

Another option: instead of simply having an either/or between the basic and advanced APIs, what if we created a template function to allow the user to select any class they want? Example:

```c++
pybind11::options options;
options.set_gil_scoped_acquire<basic_gil_scoped_acquire>();
```

Internally, this would use a virtual class with the selected class as a templated member.

We could also do something similar as a compile time option. Example:

```c++
#define PYBIND11_GIL_SCOPED_ACQUIRE basic_gil_scoped_acquire
```

And then inside pybind:

```c++
#ifndef PYBIND11_GIL_SCOPED_ACQUIRE
#define PYBIND11_GIL_SCOPED_ACQUIRE ::pybind11::gil_scoped_acquire
#endif
```

One thing is for sure: as pybind becomes more widespread, and larger and more complicated projects start using it, they too are going to encounter this issue. It is certainly having a major impact on KWIVER, so I will take it upon myself to implement any proposal we can come up with. I hope that we can find a solution that meets everyone's needs.

We ended up discovering two issues with DataDog's agent:

1) the DD agent did not properly work with Gevent in non-trivial applications (their support team acknowledged this and asked us to change our application to use prefork instead of fixing the issue) 

2) the DD agent was not picking up exceptions from custom error handlers in Django. 

We invested about 40 hours into debugging their product and had to send them example projects where their agent was not working in order to get any assistance. Their support was awful, uninformed, and unwilling to investigate anything other than common error scenarios. After providing proof, they acknowledged the issues and then asked us to rank how important it was to us to fix and properly support them.

As a result, we ended up going with New Relic, and we have been very happy with our decision. I recommend you save yourself the headache and do the same.

I don't agree with the expression **['output']**. I know it is shortcut for convenient, I used to love shortcut, but it is very difficult for new users to interpret the code.

> Shouldn't the `match` function in `get_output_shape_for` be in `__init__ `?

The idea is that `get_output_shape_for` can also process other shapes than `self.input_shape`, e.g., if you have a fully-convolutional network with some shapes specified as `None`, you can call `lasagne.layers.get_output_shape` to obtain the output shape for a particular fully-specified input shape. So we'd like to have the check in `get_output_shape_for`, not only in `__init__`.

It's still unfortunate that the error is not raised early, but only when the next layer is added or the output shape is requested. That's a more general problem, not just for the concatenation layer. We could call `self.output_shape` at the end of every constructor to ensure the shapes are sane. We cannot place this call in the super constructor, though, because at that time not all attributes may be set that are required by `get_output_shape_for`. We'd need something else in the base class that's called after instantiating the layer. I think we had a long discussion about this, but I cannot find it quickly (it may have been via email before the github repository existed). Any ideas that only require changes in the base class are welcome!

But yes, we could also have a shape check in the constructor for this specific layer.

> But just because we haven't agreed on multi-output layers yet. It doesn't require get_output() to be moved back into the Layer class.

OK -- I struggled to implement a Layer class that wraps some other layers in a nice way, it felt like having these get_* functions as methods would have made it more natural (I could override them to delegate to the equivalent methods on the layers I wrap), but if I'm missing a better way I'm not particularly hung up on it.

> seems more sensible than creating a new Layer that merges lasagne.layers.get_all_layers(output_layers), calling get_output() on it and discarding it.

It might feel a bit odd if that was the only purpose of it, but if you had something like a TupleLayer or DictLayer which is a tuple or dict of other layers, it could be useful more generally, feels like quite a natural thing to have if you support multiple outputs, and could then a useful thing to build on.

I dunno, just an idea anyway re multiple inputs/outputs. I think my angle on this is sometimes it helps a lot to find the right simple, general composable abstraction to use at the core of a library, especially if it's an abstraction that you expect to compose a lot. Sometimes it might feel like unneeded generality given the common use case, but if you do it right it actually simplifies things, and you can still add conveniences for common cases, but they're just small add-on conveniences that don't get complected with all the core logic for composition. Where there's precedent for it too that can give you confidence -- e.g. with theano's graph in this case.

```python
def __init__(self, foo, bar, target_var=None):

```
is not much more complex than:

def __init__(self, foo, bar):

Further as Arman points out, we apply the same sorting (newest first) to the `db[-5:]` so `db[-5:][-1] != db[-1]`.

The question is 'newest first' vs 'chronological order' for consuming the search result.  I think we settled on 'newest first' because sitting at the BL the more recent headers are more likely to be the relevant ones.

I am not concerned about confusion around `db[-1]` vs `db()[-1]`.  I am actually more concerned that `__call__` returns a list not a generator.

The behavior you're describing is the intended behavior.  The keys in the indexer dictionary are totally unrelated to the vocabulary namespaces.  This gives you some flexibility in sharing vocabulary namespaces across different indexers, though, honestly, I'm having a hard time right now thinking of a use case where you would actually want to do this.  But more practically, it's hard from an implementation perspective to get those two keys to always match, because they are parameters specified for different objects in the class hierarchy.

To set the namespace for an indexer you do: `token_indexers={"some_key": SingleIdTokenIndexer("some_namespace")}`.

unfortunately, there's not a way to do that right now. But you're not the first person to ask for it, so let us think about the best way to make that happen. I created #1096 to track it.

In the meantime, you have two options.

(1) write a quick script to transform your inputs into the expected JSON format:

```json
{"source": "..."}
{"source": "..."}
...
```

that's probably the easiest and what I would do.

(2) the predictor expects one input per line, but you can override the JSONL aspect, so if your inputs are just tab-delimited lines, you could create your own `Predictor` subclass that overrides `load_line`:

```python
def load_line(self, line: str) -> JsonDict:
    """you should probably use csv.reader here, but ¯\_(ツ)_/¯"""
    return {"source": line.split("\t")[0]}
```

but then you have all the extra "using allennlp in your library" steps, so I would really do (1) unless it's not feasible for some reason

My perception of (4) is the right thing will not always get done on py27 which is our most popular platform (as we haven't released anything but `acme` for py3 yet). If we're going to install `argparse` in the py2 wheel, we either:

1. Build the wheel using py26.
2. Depend on `argparse` in our sdist on py27 systems as well.

Option 1 requires people doing releases to have py26 installed on their system (which isn't the end of the world but at least I'd prefer not to) and I think option 2 is a bit of a problem. Not all systems recognize stdlib `argparse` as satisfying the `argparse` requirement. Testing on Ubuntu, Debian, CentOS, Fedora, Arch, and FreeBSD, only Debian based distros seem to do so. This means when installing from `pip`, an old version of `argparse` gets installed. For OS packages, some surgery may be required as described [here](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/pull/2249#issuecomment-259044484).

While this is unnecessary, it may not be a big deal unless PyPI `argparse` is used instead of stdlib `argparse` when it's imported. During my testing, none of the systems I tried had this behavior, however, this is the problem described in the original post. @cmskog, what OS are you using and how did you install Certbot?

Sometimes `body` can also be a string, a list or something that can be serialized into such. That makes any sort of validation unfortunately very difficult. In most cases when you pass in something wrong you will get a `SerializationError`, if it's, however, something that can correctly serialize, there is nothing we can do.

For more tighter control over the body for certain operations you can check out the dsl library - https://github.com/elastic/elasticsearch-dsl-py

Gmail uses the blue border as 'cursor' but it does selections in very bright reverse highlight.

I actually don't think that highlight needs to look particularly good, and I mean that :) In particular: highlight is, by definition, transient.  And while we don't want it to blink in pink and green stripes, the point of using very obvious colors that stand out is to flag to the user in an absolutely unmistakable way that this region is one where an action is about to happen.

So I'm not sure that a design constraint here should be "looks good & isn't a big change". I'd say the design constraint should be "highlights the region in an unmistakable way to the user, without being hideous, it's OK if the UI looks temporarily a bit odd, since it's flagging an important and fleeting condition".  

Just my take on it...

Just to summarize the work arounds related to issues with Jupyter/IPython hanging/not-responding/stuck for 5 minutes when starting/opening/creating notebook due to sqlite DB file on NFS mount:
- IPython: pass `--HistoryAccessor.hist_file` parameter when running IPython to a different path on a non-NFS mount e.g. `ipython --HistoryManager.hist_file=/tmp/ipython_hist.sqlite`, or set environment variable `IPYTHONDIR` to a non-NFS mount directory. E.g. `export IPYTHONDIR=/opt/appserver/sandbox/.ipython`
- Jupyter: Adding NotebookNotary.db_file=':memory:' in `$HOME/.jupyter/jupyter_notebook_config.py` which creates the sqlite database in memory, so will not persist when Jupyter process restarted. Or moving data directory to non-NFS partition by setting `JUPYTER_DATA_DIR` environment variable before running Jupyter. E.g. 
`export JUPYTER_DATA_DIR=/opt/appserver/sandbox/.local/share`

The following work around from [the documentation](https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/blob/715a6c02db0f6ad639d63e4f6fdff0cc63b91ec2/docs/source/security.rst) doesn't seem to work, when starting Jupyter, log said data_dir was not recognized as a valid configuration:

I was successful at getting it to install on ubuntu by doing the following:

1) Install numpy: `pip install numpy`

2) Install the packages needed to install lxml, which is a dependency of PyML.  On ubuntu this can be done as follows: ` sudo apt-get install libxml2-dev libxslt-dev python-dev`

3) Download and install PyML: 

```
curl -L http://sourceforge.net/projects/pyml/files/latest/download?source=typ_redirect > PyML.tar.gz;
tar -xzf PyML.tar.gz && cd PyML-* && python setup.py build && python setup.py install && rm -rf PyML.tar.gz; 
```

4) Install Talon: `pip install talon`

Also, you're trying to install talon inside a vagrant environment, you need to increase the amount of memory available to virtualbox to at least 1024mb in order to get PyML to install. You can do this by adding these lines to the end of  your vagrant file:

```
config.vm.provider "virtualbox" do |v|
    v.memory = 1024
    v.cpus = 2
end
```

I think it should be a context manager so you can throw state away if you want to in the middle of a test.

I think we want to use it to test flows with different kinds of user data. In order to do that, we need to "create users" that it knows about along with the users' relevant `user_info` data. Plus I think we need to be able to trigger a login flow.

So maybe something like this:

```
client = Client()

with oidc_mock as om:
    om.create_user(
        email='foo@example.com',
        user_info={
            # user info data you want passed back here
        }
    )
    om.login(client, 'foo@example.com')
```

Then `.login()` would log `foo@example.com` in by calling the appropriate things using the test client and returning the appropriate HTTP responses for requests `.get()` and `.post()` calls from the callback view.

Then you can test "new user logs in" and "existing user logs in" as well as "user logs in with xyz user info data" variations.

Does that sound like a helpful API?

@ibnesayeed and I had an extensive in-person discussion on resolving this issue.

The proposal involves revising the way `ipwb replay` is reading and processing its parameters. One issue is that if a user types simply `ipwb replay`, a default WARC is indexed and replayed.

Doing `ipwb index some.warc | ipwb replay` currently causes the replay script to read from the pipe as a source for the CDXJ to be replayed.

A common UNIX convention is to use the `-` parameter to read the piped output in receiving commands.

We will revise the index/replay systems to acts as follows:
* `ipwb replay` will cause the default WARC content to be indexed and replayed.
* `ipwb index some.warc | ipwb replay` will _also_ cause the default WARC content (_not_ some.warc) to be indexed and replayed.
* `ipwb index some.warc | ipwb replay -` will instruct the replay system to read the CDXJ result from the indexer from the pipe and replay it.
* `ipwb index some.warc | ipwb replay someOther.cdxj` will cause the replay system to ignore what is on the pipe and use `someOther.cdxj` as the basis for replay.
* `ipwb replay someOther.cdxj` will instruct the replay system to use `someOther.cdxj` as the basis for replay.

Much of this can be done in `__main__.py`.

The use case for this is that we have Python 2.7 installed on each cluster node, but we are not allowed to install additional packages. So I create an archive of the virtual environment with the required packages (as described here [http://mfcabrera.com/python/2016/04/25/python-streaming-blog-org.html](url) by @mfcabrera) and I create a custom hadoopjobrunner to load the archive and environment variables from the configuration file. The cmdenv option of hadoop streaming is used to add the packages in the virtual environment archive to the Python path.

The HadoopJobRunner:

    class AdaHadoopJobRunner(luigi.contrib.hadoop.HadoopJobRunner):
    
        def __init__(self):
            config = configuration.get_config()
            streaming_jar = config.get('hadoop', 'streaming-jar')
            cmdenvs = config.get('hadoop', 'cmdenvs', default=None)
            archives = config.get('hadoop', 'archives', default=None)
            
            if cmdenvs:
                cmdenvs = cmdenvs.split(';')
            
            if archives:
                archives = archives.split(';')
                
            super(AdaHadoopJobRunner, self).__init__(streaming_jar=streaming_jar, cmdenvs=cmdenvs, archives=archives)

I finally figured it out.
You first need to define your own callback function:
```
class MonitorCallback(tflearn.callbacks.Callback):
    def __init__(self):
        self.train_losses = []
    def on_batch_end(self, training_state, snapshot=False):
    	print("The training loss is: ", training_state.global_loss)
    	self.train_losses.append(training_state.global_loss)
```
And then you just need to initialize this class:
```
monitorCallback = MonitorCallback()
model = ...
model.fit(..., callbacks=monitorCallback)
```

The train loss will be stored in `monitorCallback.train_losses` now.

I use `on_batch_end` here, but you can also use other functions.

> How does election of the "relay node" happen? To make sure that there's always one node replicating from the original DC master?

The same way as leader election currently happens. If we are bootstrapping a new standby cluster, one node will create `/initialize` key in DCS and take a basebackup from the remote cluster. After that, it will create '/leader' key and start postgres with recovery.conf. Other nodes will wait until it becomes accessible and take a basebackup from the "fake leader".

If the /leader key expires in DCS, other nodes will do absolutely the same leader elections, but with slightly relaxed constraints (they don't care about `maximum_lag_on_failover`). The new leader will start replicating from remote cluster and other nodes will start replicating from the new leader.

> How does the system decide that the original DC is gone? Would it be possible to automate this via a consensus among three data centers, or are you doing DC failover manually?

I think that manual promotion of a standby cluster would be good enough for the beginning.

Great! As a little addendum, I'll include the relevant part of jsonschema's code that is shoving the whole schema into the error response.

    def __unicode__(self):
        essential_for_verbose = (
            self.validator, self.validator_value, self.instance, self.schema,
        )
        if any(m is _unset for m in essential_for_verbose):
            return self.message

        pschema = pprint.pformat(self.schema, width=72)
        pinstance = pprint.pformat(self.instance, width=72)
        return self.message + textwrap.dedent("""

            Failed validating %r in schema%s:
            %s

            On instance%s:
            %s
            """.rstrip()
        ) % (
            self.validator,
            _utils.format_as_index(list(self.relative_schema_path)[:-1]),
            _utils.indent(pschema),
            _utils.format_as_index(self.relative_path),
            _utils.indent(pinstance),
        )

As you can see, the str/unicode representation of the exception object is the concatenation of the error message itself _and_ the entire schema.

If Connexion were to use the inner `.message` attribute instead of the str/unicode representation, then I think we'd be good.

I couldn't sleep so I found the problem. The custom handler function is stored in `views.py` at module level scope. Since the module is already imported, the `jwt_response_payload_handler` in `views.py` is basically locked to whatever the value of `api_settings.JWT_RESPONSE_PAYLOAD_HANDLER` was at module load time.

The `setUp` method changed `api_settings` and that had no effect on the value of `jwt_response_payload_handler`.

My guess is that `APIClient` changed something about how it loads the views so that it no longer worked when settings were changed (it smells like a caching change to me).

My fix works by hotswapping the module level variable in `setUp` and restoring it in `tearDown`. I chose this route because the project does not use `mock`, and I didn't want to pull that in as a dependency.

@hdarwin 

There's really nothing wrong with Z3's answers here. First note that you're using 'Int', which has a well-defined division rule, but maybe not what you would expect. It's outlined here: http://smtlib.cs.uiowa.edu/theories-Ints.shtml#cl-52

In particular, division by 0 is underspecified, meaning that there is a model for every Int value as the result.

Under those assumptions, all the answers you get are actually valid solutions to the equation you posed.

If you want to solve this system "mathematically," you should be using the `Real` type and explicitly disallow for division-by-0 cases. The following seems to do the trick:

```
#!/usr/bin/env python
from z3 import *
s = Solver()
t = Real('t')
c = 4 / t - 5 / (t + 3)
s.add(c == 1)
s.add(t != 0)
s.add(t != -3)
while s.check() == sat:
    m = s.model()
    [s.add(d() != m[d]) for d in m.decls()]
    print m
```

I get:

```
[t = -6]
[t = 2]
```

which is I believe the results you were looking for.

More generally, there are users who would like a version of "at" to return an element instead. The nice thing with "at" is that it can be defined totally by returning an empty string/sequence when the index is out of bounds.

I have two readings of your question:

1. There is no real minimization in step 2, but rather you have a case of the form:
Given a CSP formula phi[x1,..,xn, y] and domain constraints x1 in D1, .., xn in Dn, where D1, ,.., Dn are finite set of values you want to compute the set of tuples (d1, d2, .., dn), such that d1 in D1, d2 in D2, .., dn in Dn, where phi[d1, ..,, dn, y] is satisfiable.
This is classical all-SAT or let's call it all-SMT for finite domains. If you keep blocking old solutions in a naive way Z3 will eventually suffer from memory overhead.

2. You wish to compute the maximal subset of x1, .., xn, such that the constraints x_i in D_i are true and also phi[x1,.., xn, y] is true for some y. This is a Max-SAT/Max-SMT problem. You can formulate this directly using "soft constraints". The tutorial http://rise4fun.com/z3opt elaborates on the optimization features in Z3. You basically assert phi, and you create formulas corresponding to "x_i in D_i" (a disjunction of equalities) and assert them as soft constraints.

This is clever, but I'm not sure how fast it is.

That said, it's also got a problem, because setting the `TZ` variable doesn't instantly change the results of `time.tzname` and the other things that `tzlocal` looks for, it only changes after `time.tzset` has been called, so this will fail if you did this:

```python
import os
from dateutil import tz

os.environ['TZ'] = 'PST8PDT'
local0 = tz.tzlocal()
os.environ['TZ'] = 'EST5EDT'
local1 = tz.tzlocal()
time.tzset()
local2 = tz.tzlocal()
```

In that situation, `local0`, `local1` and `local2` will all be PST8PDT, because the cache will be invalidated between `local0` and `local1`, but the actual underlying hooks will not be returning anything different, and `local2` will hit the (incorrect) cache.

Just a quick feedback on a problem related to this one that kept me blocked for days:

I followed [the tutorials for my ErleCopter on an ErleBrain3](http://docs.erlerobotics.com/simulation/configuring_your_environment) on a fresh install of Ubuntu 14.04 and ran into this precise problem with the same error message.

Trying the solutions proposed here did not work for me until I realized that what the had me install installed the `ros-indigo-mavlink` package, which installs as a dependency `pymavlink` version `1.1.73` (the faulty one). This version conflicted with the one that I installed via pip: even though the `2.0.6` version was installed, python would used ROS' version and I couldn't uninstall it using pip.

The solution was to `sudo apt-get remove --purge ros-indigo-mavlink` and `sudo pip install pymavlink==2.0.6`

Correct me if I'm wrong, but according to [spec](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6241#section-8.1) server and client don't have to wait for each other's hello messages that include their capabilities:

> Capabilities are advertised in messages sent by each peer during
   session establishment.  When the NETCONF session is opened, each peer
   (both client and server) MUST send a <hello> element containing a
   list of that peer's capabilities.
> ...
> Each peer sends its <hello> element simultaneously as soon as the
   connection is open.  A peer MUST NOT wait to receive the capability
   set from the other side before sending its own set.

Right now, it looks like ncclient is waiting for a server to send a ```hello``` message first which clearly violates the spec.

* Avoid writing the same feature functions for neurite, neuron and population classes
* avoid polution of the classes' scopes with the addition of more and more features

We don't need to do either of these. We don't need to write functions for every single feature, and there is no reason we should be writing them twice anyway. I have said many times that it doesn't scale, which is why I think the `ezy` module is a bad idea and I would like to remove it one day. What I think needs to be done is to simplify the iteration mechanisms such that a user can simply say (ignoring filtering for simplicity)

```python
stuff = [my_function(x) for x in neuron.neurites]
stuff = [my_function(x) for x in population.neurites]
```

Of course, this can be put in a helper function. The point is that there is no difference between a neuron and a population when it comes to neurites.

What is missing here is a means to bind functions to iteration modes. I have to think about how to do this nicely. Of course, the user could still say something like

```python
stuff = [my_segment_fun(s) for s in neuron.segments]
stuff = [my_segment_fun(s) for s in population.segments]
```
but it would be nice to have a mechanism by which they don't have to do that, at least for NeuroM provided functions.

Hi @jashugan 

Just to summarize our findings (and future actions on this):

1. The behavior you're seeing is the result of the (admittedly opinionated) design of transit-python at present, which will only marshall unicode strings as unicode strings, and not when encoded in byte strings.
2. End to end unicode works, which can be verified using:

  ```
  cat unicode.json  && cat unicode.json | bin/read-write && echo
  ["~#'","Av. Zañartu 1482, Ñuñoa, Santiago, Chile, 7780272"]
  ["~#'","Av. Zañartu 1482, Ñuñoa, Santiago, Chile, 7780272"]
  ```

3. Inconsistencies between transit's behavior and `json` libraries are due to the fact that transit presently uses custom json encoding to support its lazy writing method. Our plan is to eventually adopt a json dependency which will mitigate this, improve performance, and eliminate other pain points the custom encoding may be introducing, but we don't have an estimate for when this work may take place.

As such, I'm going to label this issue as an enhancement request for handling unicode encoded byte strings.

The example code found on the releases page is kept in sync with those found in the repository. The only difference, for Python 3 users, is that the examples are run through the 2to3 converter.

We haven't deeply investigated web app performance because it's a very niche use-case, so it's difficult to provide specific data on what is "normal" for that environment. I can say though that the most likely cause relates to the [Cache](https://bitbucket.org/jurko/suds/src/0fcfff1ea149397bfa23c7537a0ae2ac300e9564/suds/cache.py?at=release-0.6&fileviewer=file-view-default) or lack thereof. This can be specified when [initializing an AdWordsClient instance](https://github.com/googleads/googleads-python-lib/blob/master/googleads/adwords.py#L363).

One thing to keep in mind is that this library is dynamically generated; i.e. assuming that the cache is disabled, for every API call you make, you are downloading and parsing a WSDL to create the service objects. Even if the cache is enabled, you are still downloading and parsing a WSDL on the first API call to a particular service. Of course, the cache itself does need to be refreshed every so often, which means you will again re-download and parse the WSDL to do that. I'm not sure what, if any, caching mechanism you are using for your web app, but that is one plausible cause for worse performance.

Another possibility that I would expect to have a lesser impact is logging. If it is enabled, there will be additional processing done to handle the logs and redact certain sensitive information.

Here's the logs demonstrating what is sent correctly in [Python 2](https://pastebin.com/qZ7gV741) and incorrectly in [Python 3](https://pastebin.com/WXYqeHVT). Did you spot the difference?

It looks like the underlying issue is that bytes don't cleanly print out their contents as the equivalent str does in Python 2. To get an idea of the issue, try a trivial exercise in your Python interpreter window:

```
>>> print('hello')
hello
>>> print(b'hello')
b'hello'
```

Look closer at the Python 3 logs and you'll see that the base64 encoded data is surrounded by `b'` and `'`. Or in other words, `b''`. The suds library accepts the bytes object and simply inserts it into the generated SOAP request without any complaint.

As a work-around, you can make the following modification:
```
  image_data = base64.b64encode(opener.open(url).read()).decode('utf-8')
```

This is more of an issue with the Python 3 examples after 2to3 conversion rather than an issue with the library itself. I'll try to sneak in a fix for these examples in the next release.

Hi @raphaelquast,

Thank you for reporting this. I didn't see this issue until now -- feel free to ping me about Lambdify (you can see authors under git blame). 

I would consider this a bug. LambifyCSE should work just like Lambdify (the user should not need to pass `np.newaxis`).

To call `f_seng` in your second example I would do it this way:
```
>>> inp = np.empty(a.shape + (2,))
>>> inp[..., 0] = a
>>> inp[..., 1] = a
>>> f_seng(inp)
```
(since both x & y are "a" it's not obvious to me if this gives you what you want?)

The assumptions made for broadcasting were only dictated by cache locality (and C ordered
output array)

Actually, the way Lambdify is implemented now is that a list of length 3 is treated as matrix of shape (1, 3) in some sense, e.g.:
```
In [42]: symengine.Lambdify([x], [x, x**2, x**3])([4, 5])
Out[42]: 
array([[   4.,   16.,   64.],
       [   5.,   25.,  125.]])
```
But if we would have passed the same list to matrix it would have been interpreted as a (3, 1) shaped matrix:
```
In [44]: symengine.Matrix([x, x**2, x**3])
Out[44]: 
[x]
[x**2]
[x**3]

In [45]: symengine.Matrix([x, x**2, x**3]).shape
Out[45]: (3, 1)
```

That might be inconsistent enough that we should change what Lambdify does here.
That is: right now the extra input shape appended to the "left end" of the shape tuple, perhaps it should be the "right end" (then we should change the ordering of the output array to column major).

@PeteCausey-Freeman , @meng: I'd like to discuss how this should work. The upshot is that I think we should leave the code as-is, but I want your opinions. (Sorry Meng -- I see that you already submitted a PR.)

First, the cause (for Peter): We recently added a configuration parameter, max_ref_length, that limits the size of the deleted reference sequence displayed for del and delins. Since the deleted sequence is always implied by the reference, it's always legal to omit it. Adding the deleted sequence is particularly useful for relatively small changes. That is, we're happy to look at delACGinsTAGCAC, but less happy if the del sequence is long. We made the default max_ref_length = 0, so that the del sequence is not shown by default. This issue for identity variants occurs because the stringification for identity variants uses the same reference sequence length. With the current code, you can do this:

```Python
>>> v = hp.parse_hgvs_variant("NC_000017.11:g.50198002C=")

# by default:
>>> v.format()
u'NC_000017.11:g.50198002='

# one-time format with custom config:
>>> v.format(conf={"max_ref_length": 10})
u'NC_000017.11:g.50198002C='

# change the global setting
>>> hgvs.global_config.formatting.max_ref_length = 5
>>> v.format()
u'NC_000017.11:g.50198002C='
>>> str(v)
'NC_000017.11:g.50198002C='
```

In reviewing [the VarNomen guidelines for "no change" variants](http://varnomen.hgvs.org/bg-material/consultation/svd-wg001/), there are examples with sequence (`g.11890634G=`, `c.123G=`) and without (`NM_001849.3:c.1_*1=`, `LRG_476:g.4950_39800=`). 

Rationale for keeping the code as-is:
* Consistency: the rule for reference sequence, whether no change" or for a del, should be consistent.
* Omitting the sequence is permitted by the spec.
* It's quite easy for users to change.

So, for these reasons, I'd like to keep the code unchanged. As frequent users, I'd like your input.

A long discussion, but I think there is something positive coming out of it. It is clearly the mix-up of the execution of a bash command on the command line and shipping along a script for execution that is causing trouble.

Removing this behaviour by creating a new `Command` application is a good idea and can go ahead as soon as possible.

A few choices are then left to what the next step should be

1. Mark `Executable` for deprecation and not replace it as `Command` allows for issuing a script as 
    well (by separating the `command` and the `inputFile`.
2. Change the behaviour of `Executable` to never accept a command. Giving `exe=echo` will 
   then fail. The `exe` attribute could then become a `IGangaFile` object.
3. Combine 1. and 2. in the sense that we deprecate `Executable` and then create a new 
  application (`Program`?)  that get the behaviour of the updated `Executable`.

I am kind of in favour of option 2. but it gives some trouble in terms of schema evolution as the `exe` attribute will change type.

The user and I got to the bottom of the issue.  The problem stems from 2 things:

1. Last year he manually downloaded apache-log4j-extras and did a mvn install on it. In his local .m2 there is actually a apache-log4j-extras-source.jar file present.

2. In ivysettings.xml that I copied from the pants repo, there is a setting to try to read from the local .m2 directory:

https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/7d4202a65360e00b7b0bdb8fe9a25e72ac4e8fa7/build-support/ivy/ivysettings.xml#L66

```
      <!-- mvn standard -->
      <filesystem name="local.m2" m2compatible="true" local="true" checkmodified="true">
        <ivy pattern="${m2.repo.dir}/${m2.repo.pom}"/>
        <artifact pattern="${m2.repo.dir}/${m2.repo.artifact}"/>
```

The fix was to comment out these lines in ivysettings.xml, then remove `~/.ivy2/pants/log4j/apache-log4j-extras`  and now pants idea works for him.

Thanks @jsirois , that's definitely an interesting suggestion!

One downside I see is that it removes the trampolining that happens when we `yield` as a coroutine. `@rules` actually synchronously calling one another would put us back in a python threadstack ~all of the time. Whether this results in a net advantage in terms of time spent outside of the GIL is an open question, because if python functions were to synchronously call into other rules, I believe that the GIL would be released for the length of the call.

> Taken further (but importantly ignoring variant selects), this just means @rule becomes nothing but type annotations for a python function; ie: things eventuially could just boil down to:

I believe that it's possible that the `@rule` declaration can actually be simplified with either of the proposals, but I haven't looked into how variants will affect it either. I'd need to look at more callsites, but it's possible that `SelectVariant` would become a flavor of `Get`, which would mean that `@rule` arguments could be simplified similarly.

What you're describing is a feature of your operating system's file manager, like Windows Explorer, Finder, Nautilus, Dolphin, Thunar, etc.

If you want unique file names, you have to set them yourself as a parameter. Usually, this is being done by using a timestamp in a certain format (that can be sorted).

```bash
streamlink -o "$(date +"file_%Y%m%d%H%M%S.flv")" URL QUALITY
```

If you want the file names to have an unique ID included instead, you will need to write a script that reads all the file names of the target directory and generates a new one that doesn't exist yet depending on the existing ones.

If refactoring the dataflow is ok, there are some other aspects I would like to discuss or even rebel against:

- `reset_state` seems to be unnecessary long. Why not simply `reset`? There is no ambiguity but a high chance for typos when your mind is already pre-paring the code line.
- There should be a consistent naming: Currently, we have: `dp` for data point (which is ok) and we have `df` and `ds` for data-flow (I guess the latter is data-stream?). If would do another pass and just rename `ds` to `df`.

And the last point (not this PR) would be a guarantee to sample each data-point per epoch. As far as I understand, `Prefetch<someSuffix>` allows me to hog the entire CPU power and pre-process data. But having 12 worker processes gives me a [really high chance](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_problem) to miss some examples in one epoch when each worker shuffles data.

Ok, so I talked to base-files package maintainer, and he cleared things up for me. Because for Debian, testing and unstable use a rolling release model, they are deliberately leaving the VERSION and VERSION_ID - fields off the os-release file, because they are meant for an actual release pinpointing.

Now leutil.get_os_info() is currently only used in apache plugin to determine the distribution name from the first part of the tuple it returns, and as UA - string in client.py. So I'd leave get_os_info() as it is, and write a new method for UA string, reading the NAME - variable from os-release. That is present in all of the (systemd) distributions after all, and the ID - field is enough to figure out the configuration paradigm for other use cases.

I don't think this makes sense. The Let's Encrypt renewer doesn't set up a cron job, you as the user set up a cron job.
If you're wanting a script to run after the renewer runs I think it's best to have cron call a bash script that then calls your second command or series of commands after the renewer runs. This especially makes sense in the case where you need to worry about user permissions, since LE won't handle those well.
The only reason I can think of that this would be useful is if you needed an assurance that the renewer successfully fetched a new cert.

@vvalien Thanks for your good question.  It prompted me to add an new example "remove_unused.py".  What you want is already possible.  Code from new example is here:

```Python
import cmd2

class RemoveUnusedBuiltinCommands(cmd2.Cmd):
    """ Example cmd2 application where we remove some unused built-in commands."""

    def __init__(self):
        cmd2.Cmd.__init__(self)

        # To hide commands from displaying in the help menu, add their function name to the exclude_from_help list
        self.exclude_from_help.append('do__relative_load')

        # To remove built-in commands entirely, delete their "do_*" function from the cmd2.Cmd class
        del cmd2.Cmd.do_cmdenvironment

if __name__ == '__main__':
    app = RemoveUnusedBuiltinCommands()
    app.cmdloop()
```

You can hide commands from displaying in the help menu by adding them to the ``exclude_from_help`` list.  These commands will still exist, they just won't display in the main help menu.

You can remove unwanted commands entirely by using Python's built-in ``del``.

In general non-awaited coroutine = buggy program; it's not going to do what the user wants regardless of how we signal the error. So we have a lot of reasonable options for delivering the exception, including things that corrupt application state. (`KeyboardInterrupt` can also blindly overwrite other exceptions.) Unfortunately having a lot of options means that I'm not sure what the best choice is :-)

Regarding your second example (`raise`), I don't think it's an issue – the soonest we could detect the unawaited coroutine is when it passes through the next checkpoint, and `raise` isn't a checkpoint.

I guess lacking any strong intuition, my default would be to do the same thing we do for `KeyboardInterrupt`, because it's (a) plausible, and (b) we already know how to do it. So that would mean that we raise a special exception inside the main task as if it were cancelled (and I guess this exception should have higher priority than delivering a `Cancelled` error). (Speaking of which, it looks like currently Cancelled has higher priority than KeyboardInterrupt... I guess that's a bug. #184)

Ah, I see. Well, if you want to fork trio, it's allowed by the license, so have fun and all that. Beyond that, providing tools to build new async IO libraries is *definitely* outside trio's scope. We already have too many async IO libraries :-).

Obviously as the author of a new async IO library, I don't have a ton of credibility when arguing that people shouldn't make new async IO libraries. But it's true. For most types of libraries, it doesn't really matter how many competing versions there are, everyone can just pick their favorite and it's fine. But an async library is useless without an ecosystem of downstream libraries that integrate with it, and these incompatible ecosystems are competing for a limited pool of developer attention, so every new async library arguably makes Python as a whole weaker by splitting up developers into smaller and smaller pools. We're all better off if there's just one library that everyone uses.

I work on trio anyway because I think it has such dramatic benefits over other libraries that it can make up for this cost, and has a non-zero change of becoming that dominant library; obviously that's my ideal outcome. But if that doesn't work out, I'd rather trio fizzle out than that it become one of a dozen competing libraries that all have sucky ecosystems – at that point it wouldn't be helping anyone.

And in any case, implementing `trio/_socket.py` or `trio/_sync.py` is like 0.01% of the work involved in making a real async library, so I don't think providing reusable tools here is actually that helpful anyway. If you're just playing around, then implementing that stuff is part of the fun. And if you want to work on a serious async library that has the same low-level semantics as trio, then why not work on trio? [We're friendly :-).](https://trio.readthedocs.io/en/latest/contributing.html)
Not sure what exactly you have in mind for this, but I could just port pytest-asyncio to trio for you. That would probably be easy and it'd get you something, at least.
Should this be a new `io` package within trio, or completely separated code inside `trio_io`? (maybe the latter would convince me to help with #158).

As mentioned in #537, a UDP example would be good. This would also be a good way to demonstrate using `trio.socket` directly.

The example in that comment thread is kind of boring. Doing a dns query or ntp query would be more interesting.

Yeah, it would! I'm surprised we don't have an issue open for this already.

For those who haven't seen it, [there's some monitor examples here](https://curio.readthedocs.io/en/latest/tutorial.html#tasks), and [also check this out](https://twitter.com/swfiua/status/841465029409017856).

I do think we should use a slightly different architecture: in curio the monitor is a single piece of monolithic code that's built into the core library, has a hardcoded mechanism for connecting, and has a small fixed set of commands, which are implemented using internal, non-public APIs. It's an awesome and useful proof-of-concept/demo, and we should provide a similar experience, but I'd like to do it in a way that lets monitor implementations evolve independently from the core trio library.

A lot of the groundwork for this is already there: Trio provides lots of introspection APIs. (Probably there are some gaps, but we can sort that out as we go.) For example, [here's a little experiment in printing nice task trees from a live program](https://github.com/python-trio/trio/blob/master/notes-to-self/print-task-tree.py), and there are lots of other things you can do – show [task tracebacks](https://trio.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference-hazmat.html#trio.hazmat.Task.coro), visualizing [execution traces](https://trio.readthedocs.io/en/latest/tutorial.html#task-switching-illustrated), etc. So I think the next step here would be for someone -- could be anyone -- to start building something and see how it goes. The [curio monitor implementation is not very big](https://github.com/dabeaz/curio/blob/master/curio/monitor.py).

In the longer run I think it'd be really neat to have a monitor interface that's an IPython REPL + magic commands for stuff like introspecting the task tree. But that's going to take some non-trivial work in IPython and python-prompt-toolkit to make them async/trio friendly (CC @Carreau).

I did some more testing and found that the rounding error in Scipy’s uniform filter leads to a decrease of at most 2 in the bandpassed image. Because of the low image contrast this has surprisingly large effect on feature finding and refinement, which is enhanced by the deletion of features that overlap (`separation`). 

We should think carefully about this reproducibility test. The required csv file is about 250kB (my bad), so it has a significant effect on the repo size if we keep updating. Maybe 1 frame is enough for the find, refine and characterize tests? The linking tests could be done on random positions with a fixed seed.

We now have two reproducibility breaks since 0.3.1:

 * The change in grey dilation kernel from round to square (>95% reproducibility)
 * The rounding error change of the uniform filter.

There's not really good documentation yet regarding making a lightbox for the form. The documentation has everything you need as long as you can fill in the gaps, but it's not a really great step-by-step guide yet or anything.

In a nutshell, you do a server to server connection to get the token, which you can do using Python following [the sample here](https://github.com/AuthorizeNet/sample-code-python/blob/master/PaymentTransactions/get-an-accept-payment-page.py). Then, in your HTML page, you create an iframe, post the token to our server within the iframe so that the form that comes back in response loads up into the iframe, then put in the rest of the css and code to style that iframe and implement a close box and stuff.

Again, we don't have specific instructions, but you can see it done by examing the html and css in our [sample app](https://github.com/AuthorizeNet/accept-sample-app).

Just creating a shell alias will not be enough. However, as a quick work-around you could ask them to install a *symbolic link*:
```
ln -s gmx_mpi gmx
```

Of course, we also want to fix this within GromacsWrapper. What do you (and perhaps @whitead ) think of the idea to interpret
```
gmx_mpi:mdrun gmx:grompp
```
in the cfg file to mean that GW should really call `gmx_mpi mdrun ...` and `gmx grompp`? This would probably be the most intuitive for the user and also provide maximum flexibility for people who often have mixed serial/threaded and mpi installations.

:) yeah, there is also

[Rule of Repair](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unix_philosophy#Eric_Raymond.E2.80.99s_17_Unix_Rules)
```
Developers should design programs that fail in a manner that is easy to localize
and diagnose or in other words “fail noisily”. This rule aims to prevent incorrect
output from a program from becoming an input and corrupting the output of other
code undetected.
```

Actually, even in the robustness principle, `code that receives input should accept non-conformant input as long as the meaning is clear.`. In this very case, it is not clear if the user intend to advertise a /32 and made a mistake in the netmask, or intended to advertise a /24 but made a mistake in the network prefix.

Basically, there is no way to make the right decision here. Failing early (as in not advertising and logging that the input in incorrect/ambiguous) would allow people to fix the issue rather than having things apparently working as a side-effect.

I am not expert on AWS efficiency and it may do things in unusual ways -
you probably know better than me. However my observations are......

copy_file and copy_dir exceptions are likely to be called only in
exceptional cases anyway. so would cost be an issue here?

isdir would only call isempty if the key does not exist. So we either are
calling isempty on a file that does not exist (cheap?) or on a directory
that has no key. In the latter case it may potentially read all the keys in
the folder but at least then would return True. The alternative is to
return False which is the wrong answer! Also once it is fixed it is fixed
so next time would correctly identify it as a directory and would not call
isempty.

exists is more of an issue re cost. Also copy_dir calls fs.exists to
establish if key is missing. So maybe better to just drop this one. After
all you can easily use "exists or isempty".

I think exceptions would mostly be identical. The only difference would be
if there is both a missing hierarchy AND another exception. In this case
copy_dir would be called twice. The exception would be the same but the
exception trace would include exceptions from the first and second calls.
If that is an issue then could set a flag and move the second call outside
the except block to clear the stack trace.

If you do the following prior to your first example, does it work?

```python
ffs = fs_s3fs.S3FS('bucket')

I have the same opinion actually. It is said in the Zen of Python "Explicit is better than implicit" so if you want the filesystem to be closed, then you should close it.

I agree that 
```python
fs.open_fs("...")
atexit.register(fs.close)
``` 
is probably the most sensible option if you really don't want to use contexts.

Re: `to/from_dict`, I would actually prefer to keep those to vanilla `to/from_json`, because they are a more general form. Once you have `dict` of standard objects, it's easy to store that in many other formats (i.e. you get json/bson/yaml/pickle/mongo/postgresql/mysql/redis/memcache/etc adapters with oneliners). I don't mind having performant, specialized converters in addition (as we do), but there's value in providing general base format.

I have updated the release process notes (fba38746e7bbfbc227f64249a089e539460a7bb1) and I think it is more automated now - including the following the ``Makefile`` commands (instead of addressing things at the ``setup.py`` level):

```
dist: clean
	python setup.py sdist bdist_egg bdist_wheel
	ls -l dist

release-test: dist
	twine upload -r test dist/*
	echo "Review https://testpypi.python.org/pypi/planemo"

release:
	@while [ -z "$$CONTINUE" ]; do \
		read -r -p "Have you executed release-test and reviewed results? [y/N]: " CONTINUE; \
	done ; \
	[ $$CONTINUE = "y" ] || [ $$CONTINUE = "Y" ] || (echo "Exiting."; exit 1;)
	@echo "Releasing"
	twine upload dist/*
```

Let me know if you have specific ideas for further automation - I think a lot of the individual steps is because there is a human review check in there. It would be nice to automate tests of the the released artifacts to test PyPI for instance - but that should be a new issue I guess.

I really like this approach. It puts the project in the place of being able to distribute the raw materials, but also being able to benefit from (and share) what all of its downstream users learn from their `enhanced` cleanup processes.

I echo @NelsonMinar's point that `out.csv` on its own probably won't be sufficient for the enhancing process. I suspect we'll have cleanup rules that are context-driven (e.g. using the country to determine possible candidate languages for expansion). So both `out.csv` (content) + `conform` (context) make sense as the inputs to the process.

Additionally, it would be great if the enhancement code were really modular. I can imagine wanting to stack particular processing steps together and simplifying the process of contribution of a new step toward the cleanup pipeline. Not to mention making it easier if a downstream user only wants to run some of the enhancement steps, but not precisely as we're doing it.

Overall, really awesome!


![Computer: Enhance](https://media.giphy.com/media/yuQMFN4hQsUTe/giphy.gif)

I looked at the code and the problem appears to be here:

https://github.com/openaddresses/machine/blob/master/openaddr/conform.py#L704-L715

The us/or/portland.json source is a CSV file with `X` and `Y` as the last two fields.  Row #145573 contains 2 spurious `"`s which causes it to be parseable as CSV, but incorrectly and with an incorrect number of fields and therefore no `X` and `Y` fields.  The code referenced above assumes that the fields are there and throws an exception when no value is found.  This catches it, then percolates the exception up the stack:

https://github.com/openaddresses/machine/blob/master/openaddr/conform.py#L669-L671

I imagine that this is probably acceptable behavior since the standard error case is that the source CSV simply doesn't contain an `X` or `Y` field so why bother logging 416,426 errors when 1 is sufficient, but in this special case only a single line is in error while the rest of the file is parseable good data.

This is a welcome change but I think this needs some discussion:

#### Why is it slow in the first place?
Have you determined what causes the overhead when calling Scour for each file individually?  
* Is there anything slow in Scour or is it simply calling Python?
* Are the 40% overhead you mentioned for real-world data or for "empty" SVG files? (i.e. does the overhead matter when operating on typical files?

If something is slow in Scour I'd prefer to optimize that first

#### How to specify output file names
I'm not sure requiring a single output directory is the best way to approach this as it causes an asymmetry between processing single files and processing multiple files and would make it impossible to give precise file names to the output files. In your particular implementation it's also unclear what input files are and what the output file is (e.g. what would happen if the user types `scour in1.svg in2.svg in3.svg`? Or for that matter only `scour in1.svg in2.svg`?)

I'm not sure what the best option is myself yet, so any input is welcome.

Some possible implementations I can think of right now:
* Always expect groups of input/output files, i.e.  
  `scour -i in1.svg -o out1.svg -i in2.svg -o out2.svg`  
  which has the downside of requiring a lot of typing but would keep behavior consistent otherwise.
* Add separate options (e.g. `--outdir` and/or `--suffix`) that switch Scour into "batch processing mode" and deactivate the need for an output file name treating all unnamed parameters as input file names.  
  This could also work well alongside an `--inplace` option that would then overwrite input files (#129).

I'm also thinking about what should happen if a user calls scour like `scour *.svg`.

If replacement is not an option, first thing I would do is check if the camera is functioning properly in the [RealSense Viewer](https://github.com/IntelRealSense/librealsense/releases/download/v2.10.2/Intel.RealSense.Viewer.exe). The IMU rate in the ZR300 peripherals is fixed (I believe it was 250 FPS for the accelerometer and 200 for gyro) This means that when using callbacks you can expect to receive motion data every 4-5 ms.

OK, good news. I finally spent some time last night getting to the bottom of this damned thing, since it bit yet another person. Bad news: It's because Python (for reasons I don't care to look into) made a decision at some point (I guess it made sense at the time?) to have two kinds of unicode handling. This resulted in "narrow" and "wide" builds of Python in the 2.7 series. In Py3, this is distinction is removed.

Guess what? The wide builds, which are default on real computers (aka Linux), work fine, and the narrow builds, which seem to come on Macs, throw the error above. (I'm kidding about Linux, btw — or am I?)

There are two possible solutions here. One complicates the code but works properly. The other keeps the code simple, but means that you may have corner-case kinds of errors parsing documents on narrow builds (aka Macs). I've chosen to keep the code simple, log an error at import time on narrow builds, and to move on. 

If you're curious if you have a narrow build:

    import sys
    if sys.maxunicode == 65535:
        print "You have a narrow build. Bummer."

The corner case error that you may run into is that if you are parsing documents that contain characters in the following range...

    u'\U00010000-\U0010FFFF'

...they won't get removed from the document before parsing. That means that the XML parser will fail since these are invalid characters in XML. The solution at that point will be to either:

 - remove those bytes in your own pre-parser;
 - compile and install a wide build of Python; or
 - not parse the document in the first place.

My thinking on this was forged by [this answer on Stackoverflow](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/31603075/how-can-i-represent-this-regex-to-not-get-a-bad-character-range-error).

Available in `libfv` branch of the repository.  Here are a minimal set of docs for you to kick the tires:

```python
from fontv.libfv import FontVersion

# starting version string = "Version 1.000;metadata"
fv = FontVersion("path/to/font")

# ACCESS VERSION STRING DATA
version_substring = fv.version
# or
version_substring = fv.version_string_parts[0]

# CHECK STATUS INDICATOR (i.e. DEV/RELEASE/sha1-dev/sha1-release)
if fv.contains_status():
    if fv.is_development:
        # contains a development status label
    elif fv.is_release:
        # contains a release status label
else:
    # no status indicator

# ACCESS VERSION STATUS SUBSTRING DATA
status_substring = fv.status
# or if known to be set
status_substring = fv.version_string_parts[1]

# ADD DEV/RELEASE SUBSTRING DATA
fv.set_development_status()
print(fv.get_version_string())
# prints "Version 1.000;DEV;metadata"
fv.set_release_status()
print(fv.get_version_string())
# prints "Version 1.000;RELEASE;metadata"

# REPLACE STATUS LABEL WITH GIT COMMIT SHA1 HASH STATUS SUBSTRING
fv.set_git_commit_sha1()
print(fv.get_version_string())
# prints "Version 1.000;[sha1];metadata"
fv.set_git_commit_sha1(development=True)
print(fv.get_version_string())
# prints "Version 1.000;[sha1]-dev;metadata"
fv.set_git_commit_sha1(release=True)
print(fv.get_version_string())
# prints "Version 1.000;[sha1]-release;metadata

# CLEAR ALL METADATA FROM VERSION STRING
fv.clear()
print(fv.get_version_string())
# prints "Version 1.000"

# SET THE VERSION NUMBER
fv.set_version_number("2.000")
print(fv.get_version_string())
# prints "Version 2.000"

# MANUALLY SET THE ENTIRE VERSION STRING
fv.set_version_string("Version 3.000;metadata")
print(fv.get_version_string())
# prints "Version 3.000;metadata"

# WRITE TO FONT BINARY -  on filepath used for FontVersion instantiation by default
fv.write_version_string()
# path/to/font now has version string "Version 3.000;metadata"

# WRITE TO FONT BINARY - new filepath
fv.write_version_string(fontpath="test/NewFont-Regular.ttf")
# test/NewFont-Regular.ttf now has version string "Version 3.000;metadata"
```

I'm going to merge this.

It should contain any regressions, as a normal system that doesn't impose any conflicting libraries with our bundled ones won't be affected... so for most cases of our currently running deployments, the change should go unnoticed.

One pitfall would be in case a system has `OpenSSL` and `cryptography` (they will always both be present as latter is a dependency of the former): Before, things might have run smoothly, because under the hood, both system packages were being used... after this path change, we risk a case where our bundled `cryptography` takes precedence... risking that it's incompatible with the system's `OpenSSL`. See errors reported by @lyw07. I'm willing to gamble that :)

Just to be clear on the relevance for 0.7:

On the Debian installer, we currently have issues that an installed `python3-cryptograhy` will interfere (with Morango) and break Kolibri because Ubuntu (16.04) ships with 1.2.3. This will at least be fixed. I haven't checked up on the relevance of this Python 3 package, but it's a dependency of `python3-openssl`.

Furthermore, we have issues that installing `python3-openssl` will break the package, and for `python-openssl`, it will break both Pex (normally runs with system libraries) and normal .whl packages (which can run in virtualenvs and be isolated... or not).

For all of us, and people integrating Kolibri in more advanced deployments, it's nice to get on track with this so the issue won't show up breaking things in some later production critical scenario.

Ah, and for the subsequent issues: We need to work out what to do with `cryptography`. Bundling our own, we risk these incompatibilities, and expanding with more bundled compiled libraries is IMO the wrong path.. we can't bundled OS*ARCH*PYTHON_VERSION times compiled libraries, it doesn't scale.

The documentation is missing for that parameter. We should definitely fix that.

1. You example won't work, because properties can't have arguments.
2. You can pass user_id into schema via context:
```python
class GameSchema(marshmallow.Schema):
    is_subscribed = marshmallow.fields.Method('get_subscribed')

    def get_subscribed(self, game):
        return game.user_id == self.context.get('user_id')

schema = GameSchema()
schema.context['user_id'] = flask.current_user
schema.dump(game)
```

Also, it is possible to update dumped result afterwards:
```python
games_data = GameSchema(many=True).dump(games)
for game_data in games_data:
    game_data['is_subscribed'] = is_user_subscribed_to_game(user_id, game_data['id'])
return games_data
```

I found the root cause of the issue. The issue is the uses of the replaces attribute in migrations 0001...0005. That signifies that these migrations were squashed at some point, and when that is the case, the recorder `self.migration_qs.filter(app=app, name=name).delete()` in migrate backwards doesn't do anything. As this is a package, I will propose a pull request that deletes the 'replaces' lines, which is a moot point.

Because social_django depends on settings.AUTH_USER_MODEL reverting migrations causes a failure once the migrations are attempted to be "forwarded" again because these 5 db entries in the django_migrations table don't get deleted.

def export_data_to_gcs(dataset_name, table_name, destination):
    bigquery_client = bigquery.Client()
    dataset = bigquery_client.dataset(dataset_name)
    table = dataset.table(table_name)
    job_name = str(uuid.uuid4())
    logger.info("Created Job id for Big query : %s" %(job_name))
    job = bigquery_client.extract_table_to_storage(job_name, table, destination)
    job.destination_format = 'CSV'
    job.Compression = 'GZIP'
    job.print_header = False
    job.begin()
    wait_for_job(job)
    logger.info(" Exported %s : %s  to %s "  % (dataset_name, table_name, destination))

def wait_for_job(job):
    logger.info("Waiting for the job :%s to Finish" %(job.name))
    logger.info(" job %s created at %s " %(job.name, job.created))
    while True:
        job.reload()
        if job.state == 'DONE':
            logger.info("Job %s started at :%s  and ended at: %s " %(job.name, job.started, job.ended))
            if job.error_result:
                raise RuntimeError(job.error_result)
            return
        time.sleep(1)

What are your thoughts on if this merits a fix/improvement in gitpython?

IMHO, this is something that does merit a change.  By least-surprise, i think the user rightly expects that IFF repo does not implement context-manager, then it doesn't have side effects like persistent ownership of the repo.   "Caching" is this case is not something that should have visible effects (beyond performance) for the user.  And exposing `gc.collect()` as part of the 'cleanup api' is _definitely_ not least-surprise.  

Many ways to solve this; it seems to me that adding a context manager (or at least a "release()" function) might be the most usable, and would would make the behavior discoverable.

As a minor side point, i wasn't able to find any documentation that would definitively indicate clear_cache was actually necessary here, i just guessed as it was the closest thing to 'cleanup()' i could find.

I found the solution, very simple.
My 'custom' template was only adding some very small amount of additional info to change_list.html, so basically it was the same structure and style as the original template + some aditions: basic HTML.
Before, as the source, I used the template from **contrib/admin/templates/admin**
The solution was to use the overridden template from **django_admin_bootstrapped/templates/admin**/change_list.html: so I copied this file in **templates/admin/my_app/my_model**, then I added my stuff in that file.

More generally, all the date  information must be displayed in human readable format.

What is done about this feature: 
 - the WebUI configuration file allows to define a time zone for the Web inteface
 - each element got from the backend is stored internally and its date attributes are stored internally as UTC timestamps
 - each date attribute can be easily displayed in human readable format
